exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 1: Put the verb in brackets into the correct tense

questions.grammarRule: Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) links a finished action to now, without saying exactly when it happened. 'Already' signals the action is done. Examples: 'I have already eaten.' / 'She has already left.'

Present Perfect ('has been') is used with unspecified time expressions like 'twice'; Past Simple ('went') is used with a specific past time marker like 'last year'. Both are accepted here depending on which half of the sentence you're matching. Examples: 'I have been there twice.' / 'I went there last year.'

promptParts.before: By the time we arrived, the film

questions.grammarRule: Past Perfect (had + past participle) describes an action that finished before another action in the past — here, the film starting happened before 'we arrived'. Examples: 'The train had left when I got to the station.' / 'She had already gone to bed when we called.'

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 2: Choose the correct conditional form

promptParts.after: more time, I would learn another language.

questions.grammarRule: Second conditional ('If + past simple, ... would + infinitive') describes an unreal or unlikely present/future situation — the speaker doesn't actually have more time. Examples: 'If I had a car, I would drive to work.' / 'If she won the lottery, she would travel the world.'

promptParts.after: tomorrow, we will cancel the picnic.

questions.grammarRule: First conditional ('If + present simple, ... will + infinitive') describes a realistic future possibility. Examples: 'If it rains, I will take an umbrella.' / 'If you study, you will pass the exam.'
